Summary: This paper presents FlashAttention-2, an optimized implementation of exact attention that addresses inefficiencies in the original FlashAttention. The work introduces three main improvements: (1) algorithmic tweaks to reduce non-matmul FLOPs via delayed rescaling and logsumexp storage, (2) parallelization along the sequence length dimension to improve GPU occupancy for long sequences with small batches, and (3) improved warp-level work partitioning that splits Q instead of K/V to eliminate inter-warp shared memory communication. Empirical results on A100 GPUs demonstrate approximately 2× speedup over FlashAttention, reaching 50-73% of theoretical maximum FLOPs/s, with end-to-end GPT-style training achieving up to 225 TFLOPs/s per A100 (72% MFU).
Strengths: Substantial practical impact: a 2× speedup on a ubiquitous primitive (attention) directly translates to meaningful training cost reductions across the entire field, and 72% MFU approaches the efficiency of hand-tuned GEMM kernels.

Careful hardware-aware analysis: the paper correctly identifies that non-matmul FLOPs execute 16× slower than matmul FLOPs on A100 Tensor Cores, and derives optimizations directly from this observation.

The Q-splitting warp partitioning insight (vs. FlashAttention's K/V split) is an elegant contribution that eliminates inter-warp communication in the forward pass, with a clear mechanistic explanation.

Comprehensive empirical evaluation spanning microbenchmarks (varying sequence length, head dimension, causal mask) and end-to-end training at two model scales, with meaningful comparisons to PyTorch, FlashAttention, and Triton baselines.
Results are reproducible in practice: the CUTLASS implementation has been widely adopted in open-source libraries (xformers, HuggingFace), demonstrating real-world utility beyond the paper.
Weaknesses: Limited conceptual novelty: the contributions are primarily engineering refinements (loop reordering, rescaling deferral, work partitioning) rather than new algorithmic ideas — the underlying online softmax + tiling framework is inherited from FlashAttention.

Evaluation is restricted to a single GPU generation (A100); H100 results are mentioned anecdotally (335 TFLOPs/s) but without the TMA/4th-gen Tensor Core optimizations that would stress-test the design on newer hardware. No AMD or alternative accelerator validation.

The paper lacks ablation studies isolating the contribution of each of the three improvements (non-matmul FLOP reduction, seqlen parallelism, Q-split warp partitioning). Readers cannot determine which change drives the speedup.

Numerical precision and stability are not discussed — delayed rescaling and logsumexp reformulation could have subtle accuracy implications under FP16/BF16 that merit empirical verification (e.g., end-to-end loss curves or per-token logit deviation vs. reference).

The backward-pass description is terse: atomic additions to dQ are mentioned but contention costs, alternative schemes, and the resulting impact on backward throughput relative to forward are not quantified.
